coredata.dc:title: Multicolorimetric and fluorometric dual-modal immunosensor for histamine via enzyme-enabled metallization of gold nanorods and inner filter effect of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Shuang-Zi
Yang, Jin-Yi
Jia, Bao-Zhu
Wang, Hong
Chen, Zi-Jian
Wei, Xiao-Qun
Shen, Yu-Dong
Lei, Hong-Tao
Xu, Zhen-Lin
Luo, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Histamine (HA) is one of the key causative agents associated with allergies and food poisoning. The accurate and convenient detection of HA is highly required to avert HA toxicity and ensure food safety. Herein, a multicolorimetric and fluorometric dual-readout immunosensor for HA detection was developed based on an alkaline phosphatase (ALP) enabled multicolor change reaction and fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) resulting from the metallization of gold nanorods (Au NRs). In this immunosensor, ALP is employed as labeling enzyme which can efficiently catalyze the hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate into ascorbic acid (AA). Then, the resultant AA can reduce Ag+ into silver monomer, and a silver shell is deposited on the surface of Au NRs to form Au/Ag core-shell nanorods (Au NRs@Ag). This leads to the blue-shift of the longitudinal local surface plasmon resonance peak of Au NRs and a vivid multicolor variation. Meanwhile, the ﬂuorescence of CDs can be gradually quenched via inner-filter effect by the resultant Au NRs@Ag. Multicolorimetric assay allows a semiquantitative detection with a visible detection limit of 0.25 mg/L, and fluorometric assay provides a sensitive and accurate quantitative detection with a detection limit of 0.08 mg/L. The immunosensor not only fulfills the reliable and accurate detection requirements but also fits different testing needs for both laboratory and on-site detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Immunosensor
Inner filter effect
Carbon dots
Gold nanorod
Histamine

Characterization of CDs and Au NRs
Mechanism of the multicolorimetric and fluorescence dual-modal immunosensor
Performance of the dual-modal multicolorimetric and fluorometric immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs; (B) HRTEM image of CDs; (C) size distribution of CDs; (D) Fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (black) spectrum of CDs; (E) TEM image of Au NRs; (F) UV–vis spectrum of Au NRs.

(A) UV–vis spectra of (a) Au NRs, (b) AAP, (c) Ag+, (d) ALP, (e) Au NRs + AAP + Ag+, (f) Au NRs + AAP + ALP, (g) Au NRs + Ag+ + ALP, (h) Au NRs + AAP + Ag+ + ALP. (B) TEM images for different concentration of AA (0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90, 120 μM) used to induce Ag deposition on Au NRs; the insets are photos of corresponding colors of Au NRs solution. TEM images of Au NRs (C) before and (D) after Ag deposition induced by AA, along with the corresponding EDX element mapping image of Au and Ag and the EDX spectra of corresponding areas (E) and (F).

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, and UV–vis spectra of Au NRs after using series concentration of AA (0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90, 120 μM) used to induce metallic silver shell deposition reaction. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in response to the silver deposition induced by series concentration of AA (0–120 μM). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs + Ag+, CDs + AA, CDs + AA+Ag+, CDs + AuNRs, and CDs + AA+Ag++AuNRs. (D) Zeta potential of the prepared CDs, Au NRs and Au NRs@Ag. (E) The fluorescence recovering of CDs from Au NRs@Ag before (a) and after (b) the centrifugation. (F) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs in response to the silver deposition induced by series concentration of AA (0–120 μM).

(A) Photographs of the multi-colorimetric at different concentrations of HA (0–10 mg/L). (B) UV–vis spectra and (C) ﬂuorescence spectra for different concentrations of HA. Calibration curve based on (D) the blue shift value of LSPR band and (E) fluorescence intensity.
Specificity of the dual-modal immunosensor against histamine, histidine, tryptophane, tryptamine, tyramine and phenethylamine (spiked concentrations:1 mg/L).
Schematic illustration of the multi-colorimetric and fluorometric dual-modal immunosensor for histamine.
Analytical results of histamine in codfish and pork samples by the dual-modal immunosensor (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ND, not detected. Data were below the LOD of the method.

head.ce:title: Multicolorimetric and fluorometric dual-modal immunosensor for histamine via enzyme-enabled metallization of gold nanorods and inner filter effect of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biology and Food Engineering, Guangdong University of Education, Guangzhou 510303, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Histamine (HA) is one of the key causative agents associated with allergies and food poisoning. The accurate and convenient detection of HA is highly required to avert HA toxicity and ensure food safety. Herein, a multicolorimetric and fluorometric dual-readout immunosensor for HA detection was developed based on an alkaline phosphatase (ALP) enabled multicolor change reaction and fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) resulting from the metallization of gold nanorods (Au NRs). In this immunosensor, ALP is employed as labeling enzyme which can efficiently catalyze the hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate into ascorbic acid (AA). Then, the resultant AA can reduce Ag+ into silver monomer, and a silver shell is deposited on the surface of Au NRs to form Au/Ag core-shell nanorods (Au NRs@Ag). This leads to the blue-shift of the longitudinal local surface plasmon resonance peak of Au NRs and a vivid multicolor variation. Meanwhile, the ﬂuorescence of CDs can be gradually quenched via inner-filter effect by the resultant Au NRs@Ag. Multicolorimetric assay allows a semiquantitative detection with a visible detection limit of 0.25 mg/L, and fluorometric assay provides a sensitive and accurate quantitative detection with a detection limit of 0.08 mg/L. The immunosensor not only fulfills the reliable and accurate detection requirements but also fits different testing needs for both laboratory and on-site detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine (HA) is one of the most toxic biogenic amines closely associated with allergies and food poisonings (Gould et al., 2013; Yadav et al., 2019). Fermented foods and deteriorated protein-rich foods are inclined to be contaminated by high level of HA (Kovacova-Hanuskova et al., 2015; Tan et al., 2019). To ensure human health, maximum residue limits (MRL) ranging from 50 to 1000 mg/kg had been stipulated for HA in fish products by different countries and communities (Huang et al., 2010; Nei et al., 2017). Hence, rapid and accurate detection of HA is of paramount importance for food safety and human well-being. To date, numerous analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (Tahmouzi et al., 2011), liquid chromatography tandem-mass spectrometry (Kosma & Badeka, 2021), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Kamankesh et al., 2021), enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Xu et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2019) and biosensors (Dong et al., 2017; Venkatesh et al., 2020), have been employed for the detection of HA, but the accuracy, sensitivity and convenience of HA detection are continuing to be chased.

ELISA has been extensively taken as a gold standard for biomedical diagnosis, food safety detection and environmental analysis since first introduced in 1971. Nevertheless, improving the performance and broadening the applicability of ELISA are continuously being pursued to meet the ongoing needs of accuracy, sensitivity and simplicity. Confronting with the ever-increasing needs, recent advances in nanomaterials have provided numerous opportunities for improving the performance and widening the application of ELISA (Liu, et al., 2019). As a colorimetric immunoassay, classic ELISA usually suffers from moderate sensitivity and accuracy of visual detection due to the monocolorimetric readout, which confines the application of ELISA in resource-constrained region for on-site detection (Wang et al., 2020).

In the past decades, gold nanocrystals (Au NCs) have attracted considerable attention in the field of colorimetric sensor due to their extraordinary and tunable localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) property (Ma et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). This property makes their solution exhibit high extinction coefficients (108–109 M−1 cm−1) in visible range and present varied color dependent on their shape, size and composition (Cathcart & Chen, 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Many colorimetric sensors have been constructed based on the morphology transformation of Au NCs through etching, aggregation or growth (Gao et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2014; Xianyu et al., 2015). Among these Au NCs, gold nanorods (Au NRs) have a longitudinal and a transverse LSPR peaks, which are highly sensitive to the variation of their morphology, especially the longitudinal LSPR peak (Wang et al., 2018). Minor variations in the aspect ratio of Au NRs will induce distinct LSPR peak shifts, thus generating a series of varied colors just like a rainbow (Tian et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2018). Since the color discrimination is usually easier than the judgement of monochromatic intensity for normal human eyes, the accuracy of multicolorimetric assay for visual detection is significantly enhanced (Zhang et al., 2020). As a result, some strategies have been explored to develop multicolorimetric immunosensor by integrating ELISA platform with enzymatically-induced color change of Au NRs in the past years (Ma et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2017). However, time-consuming absorption spectrum scanning and calculation of shifts value of LSPR peaks are required to obtain accurate quantitative results via these multicolorimetric immunosensors, which limits their broader application. In recent years, dual- or multi-modal assay enabled by single or multi-signal probes have gained increasing interest due to the separate response signals and mutually confirmed analysis results which evidently enhance the accuracy and reliability of the test (Zhang et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). Hence, it is highly desired to introduce another signal readout into these Au NRs-based multicolorimetric immunoassay platform for accurate quantitative detection.

Inner filter eﬀect (IFE) is an important non-irradiation energy conversion model of fluorometry, which represents a fluorescence quenching phenomenon due to the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light of fluorophores by absorbers (quenchers) in the detection system (Liu et al., 2021). It occurs effectively when the absorption band of the absorber has a complementary overlap with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore (Li et al., 2016), which has become an efficient strategy for developing fluorescent sensors via screening and pairing different absorber and fluorescent probes (Chen et al., 2018). Because the changes in the absorbance of absorber convert into exponential variations in the fluorescence intensity of fluorophore, the detection sensitivity of the fluorimetry is significantly improved in comparison with original colorimetry (Chen et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2019; Ni et al., 2020). In the past decades, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attentions due to their superior optical properties, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2019). These desired properties make CDs become competitive substitutes to traditional quantum dots and hold great promise in fluorescence sensing applications. To date, many fluorescent sensors based on IFE of CDs have been developed for detection of various molecules (Dong et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016; Tong et al., 2020).

In fact, Au NCs can act as excellent colorimetric indicators and quenchers for constructing both colorimetric and fluorescent sensors due to their tunable plasmonic adsorption with high extinction coefficient in the visible range. Up to now, Au NRs have been applied to develop several fluorescent sensors by virtue of their exceptional quenching ability to some fluorescent materials including rhodamine 6G, upconversion nanoparticles and near-infrared-emitting luminescent nanoparticles (Ge et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020). However, the tunable plasmonic properties of Au NRs was ignored in these fluorescent sensors since the fluorescence quenching ability of Au NRs was tuned only through adjusting the distance between Au NRs and fluorophore. Therefore, Au NRs used in these sensors could not behave as colorimetric indicator. In fact, the fluorescence quenching ability of Au NRs to fluorophores also can be tuned by tuning the tunable plasmonic adsorption of Au NRs through controlling the morphology transformation of Au NRs. For this strategy, Au NRs can both act as a tunable quencher and colorimetric indicator for constructing multicolorimetric and fluorometric dual-modal sensors. Herein, a dual-modal multicolorimetric immunosensor with complementary fluorometric detection for HA was firstly tailored. To achieve this design, Au NRs was used as both the plasmonic transducer for multicolorimetric assay and the absorber for IFE-based fluorometric assay. Additionally, luminescent CDs of which fluorescent excitation and emission spectrum were both located at the tunable range of Au NRs’ LSPR absorption band was employed as the fluorescent signal source for fluorometric assay.
Materials and instrumentals are presented in Supplementary Materials.

The Au NRs was synthesized by a seed-mediated growth method as previously described with some modification (Tian et al., 2020). Briefly, the seed solution was obtained by mixing HAuCl4 (10 mM, 250 μL) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) (0.2 M, 5 mL) in a glass vial. Then, freshly prepared ice cold NaBH4 (10 mM, 600 μL) was dropwise added into the above mixture under magnetic stirring. After 2 min, the seed solution was kept at 30 °C for 1 h for further use. The growth solution was prepared by adding AA (0.1 M, 1.3 mL) to a solution containing CTAB (0.1 M, 200 mL) and HAuCl4 (10 mM, 10 mL); and then the above prepared seed solution was added and kept for 12 h in a water bath at 30 °C. The as-prepared Au NRs were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,748 g and suspended into ultra-pure water. Then, the as-prepared Au NRs were suspended in CTAB (0.06 M, 40 mL) after centrifuged twice at 107 g for 15 min and store at 4 °C.

A green-emissive CDs was prepared according to a previous literature (Li et al., 2016). Briefly, ethylenediamine (300 μL) was dissolved in a catechol (0.1 M, 25 mL) solution with the assistant of ultrasonic (25 kHz, 5min, room temperature). Then, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a 50 mL Teﬂon-lined autoclave. The obtained mixture was further diluted by 20 mL of water and centrifuged at 15,477 g for 10 min, and then the supernatant was collected and dialyzed against distill water through a dialysis a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) for 6 h. The as-synthesized CDs was kept from light at 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of multicolorimetric and fluorometric dual-modal immunosensor for HA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100 μL of HA coating antigen (1 μg/mL) in carbonate buffer (50 mM, pH 9.6) was introduced into each well of a 96-microwell plate and incubated at 4 °C for 12 h. The plate was then washed with washing buffer twice, and blocked with 120 μL of BSA solution (1%, w/v) at 37 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, 50 μL of HA standard with various concentrations or sample solution (in PBS buffer) was added, followed by adding 50 μL of anti-HA monoclonal antibody (anti-HA mAb). After incubation at 37 °C for 40 min, the plate was washed five times with washing buffer. Next, 100 μL of ALP-Ab2 (diluted 300-fold with tris-HCl buffer) was injected into each well and maintained at 37 °C for 30 min, followed by a washing step with washing buffer (five times). Afterward, 100 μL of Tri-HCl buffer (0.05 M, pH 9.7) containing 1 mM ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) was injected to each well and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. Then, a mixture of 7 μL Au NRs solution, 3 μL AgNO3 (20 mM) and 65 μL DEA buffer (0.4 M, pH9.7) was added to each well and maintained at room temperature for 20 min and terminated by 30 μL Na3VO4 (10 mM). The photograph and the UV–vis spectra were recorded by a digital camera and a multifunctional microplate reader (Spectra Max i3x, Molecular Devices, USA). Finally, 30 μL of CDs (10 μg/mL) was added to each well and shaken for 30 s; and then the fluorescent intensity at 515 nm was measured under an excitation of 410 nm by microplate reader mentioned above.

For the measurements of codfish and pork samples, samples were pretreated according to our previous work (Luo et al., 2019) with a modified dilution factor by working buffer. For recovery test, negative samples (confirmed by a LC-MS/MS method established in our previous work (Luo et al., 2014)) weighted 10 g (±0.1 g) were spiked with 20 μL, 50 μL and 100 μL of HA standard solution (10 mg/mL), respectively; and then the samples were vibrated at 4 °C for 30 min to obtain HA spiked sample at the level of 20 mg/kg, 50 mg/kg and 100 mg/kg, respectively. Samples spiked with HA or not were subjected to the following pretreatment steps. Briefly, a 2 g portion of sample was mixed with an equivalent weight of distilled water and homogenized; and then 1 g of the homogenate was added to 19 mL of PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) and vibrated for 1 min. Next, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 g for 10 min. After removing the fat layer, the supernatant was tested with the above method. To validate the accuracy and reliability of this dual-modal immunosensor, the LC-MS/MS was also used to determine the HA content of the spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and Au NRs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), respectively. The methodology of TEM, XPS, and FT-IR were presented in Supplementary materials. As shown in the TEM image of CDs (Fig. 1 A), the CDs was uniform with an averaged diameter of ∼2.3 nm (Fig. 1C). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1B indicted the high crystallinity of the CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was in accordance with that of the (100) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon. FT-IR and high resolution XPS showed the surface of CDs possessed hydroxyl, carbonyl group and amine group (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). Strong green photoluminescence was observed for the CDs solution under ultraviolet light (365 nm), and its maximum emission wavelength was 515 nm under an excitation wavelength of 410 nm (Fig. 1D). The as-synthesized Au NRs was characterized by TEM and ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis). As shown in Fig. 1E, the rod-shaped Au NRs displayed good mono-dispersity with an average width and length of 14 ± 1 nm and 52 ± 3 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the two absorption peaks at ∼515 and ∼727 nm (Fig. 1F) were well matched with the typical transverse and longitudinal LSPR peaks of previously reported Au NRs (Tian et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the multicolorimetric and fluorescence dual-modal immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the principle of the dual-modal multicolorimetric and fluorometric immunosensor for HA. Concretely, the coating antigen was precoated on the 96-well microplate, which can compete with free HA in sample solution for binding with the anti-HA monoclonal antibody (anti-HA mAb, Ab1). Thus, lower concentration of HA in sample will result in more ALP-labeled secondary antibody (ALP-Ab2) binding on the microplate. Then, the ALP on the microplate can catalyze the hydrolysis of AAP into ascorbic acid (AA).

For multicolorimetric detection, the resultant AA reduce Ag+ into silver monomer, which in situ deposits to form silver shell on Au NRs. With increasing of the thicknesses of the silver shell, the resultant Ag/Au shell-core nanorods (Au NRs@Ag) exhibits distinct color variation from pink to blue, brownish-grey, brownish-red and orange accompanied by a continuous blue-shift of the longitudinal LSPR peak. As a result, a multicolorimetric detection by naked-eye for qualitative or semiquantitative detection of HA can be achieved. To validate the feasibleness of AA-induced silver deposition on Au NRs, several control tests were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 A (curve a), Au NRs solution was pink with a longitudinal plasmon peak at 727 nm, while UV–vis absorption spectra of AAP, Ag+ or ALP solution (curve b, c and d) exhibit no obvious absorbance peaks in the visible region (400 nm–800 nm). When the mixture of AAP/Ag+, AAP/ALP or Ag+/ALP were added to Au NRs solution, the color was not changed with no shift of longitudinal LSPR peaks (curve e, f and g). Nevertheless, with the introduction of ALP to the mixture of Au NRs/AAP/Ag+, the solution color notably varied from pink to orange, and the longitudinal LSPR peak shift from 727 nm to 524 nm accompanied by the formation of a new absorption peak at 410 nm which fits with the distinctive absorption peak of silver colloids (curve h) (Gao et al., 2014). TEM image displayed that the transverse size of the as-produced Au NRs gradually increased and the solution color changed from pink to blue, brownish-grey, brownish-red and orange after the addition of AA increased from 0 to 120 μM. Moreover, the energy dispersive X-ray elemental mapping images and spectra of Au NRs before (Fig. 2C and E) and after (Fig. 2D and F) the deposition reaction further confirmed that AA-induced silver deposition on the Au NRs.

For fluorescence detection, CDs was introduced as fluorophore and the formed Au NRs@Ag acted as absorber. The excitation spectrum of CDs was centered at ∼410 nm which fairly overlapped with the silver shell's absorption spectrum of Au NRs@Ag. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the overlap between the excitation/emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectrum of Au NRs@Ag steadily expands with the blue shift of the longitudinal LSPR and the growth of silver shell's absorption peak resulting from the AA-induced silver deposition reaction. Accordingly, the fluorescence of CDs was gradually quenched (Fig. 3B). Since the immunoassay was based a competitive format, a “signal-on” fluorescence quantification of HA was achieved. To verify the feasibility of IFE-induced quenching of CDs, further investigations were performed. As shown in Fig. 3C, the individual addition of Ag+, AAP or ALP to CDs solution has little effect on the fluorescence intensity. However, Au NRs can quench the fluorescence of CDs to some extent, which might be ascribed to the intrinsic absorption to the excitation/emission light of CDs by Au NRs. As displayed in Fig. 3A, there exists a certain extent of overlap between absorption spectrum of Au NRs (before silver deposition) and the excitation/emission spectra of CDs. After silver deposition on Au NRs (in the presence of the mixture of Au NRs, ALP, AAP and Ag+), the fluorescence of CDs was further quenched due to the enlargement in the overlap region between the absorption spectrum of Au NRs@Ag and the excitation/emission spectra of CDs. The zeta potential measurement indicated that Au NRs, Au NRs@Ag and CDs were negatively charged (Fig. 3D), implying the absence of electrostatic interaction between them. Additionally, the fluorescence of CDs solution recovered after removing the Au NRs@Ag from CDs solution via centrifugation, ruling out the possibility of formation of fluorescent complex between CDs and Au NRs@Ag (Fig. 3E). Moreover, fluorescence lifetime of CDs showed no discernible variation (from 2.69 ns to 2.55 ns) in response to the silver deposition on Au NRs induced by series concentrations (0–120 μM) of AA (Fig. 3F). These results demonstrated that IFE, instead of FRET or other dynamic quenching mechanism, was the dominant reason for the fluorescence quenching of CDs (Liu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the dual-modal multicolorimetric and fluorometric immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the optimal detection performance, several critical parameters were optimized. For multi-colorimetric assay, the development of multicolor signal was stemmed from the AA-induced Ag deposition reaction controlled by the ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis of AAP. Thus, the concentration of AAP, the concentration and pH of Tris-HCl buffer, the volume of DEA buffer (0.4 M) and silver deposition time were optimized for the chromogenic reaction. As shown in Fig. S2, the optimal chromogenic conditions were as follows: AAP of 1 mM, Tris-HCl (0.05M, pH 9.7), 65 μL of DEA buffer (pH9.7, 0.4M) and deposition for 20 min. Moreover, checkerboard titration was performed to optimize the working concentration of coating antigen and anti-HA mAb for fluorescent immunoassay. As displayed in Fig. S3, 1.0 μg/mL of HA induced the highest increase rate in fluorescence signal (102.9%) when the coating concentration and the working concentration for anti-HA mAb is at 1.0 μg/mL and 2.5 μg/mL, respectively. Therefore, 1.0 μg/mL of coating antigen and 2.5 μg/mL of anti-HA mAb were selected.

Under the optimized conditions, a dual-modal immunosensor with multicolorimetric and fluorometric assay was conducted for HA. The colorimetric assay was achieved by observing the color change and shift of LSPR band, which is resulting from the immune reaction-mediated silver deposition on the surface of Au NRs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a distinct color change from orange to brownish-red to brownish-grey to blue and to light-blue can be visually observed with the increase of HA concentration from 0 to 10 mg/L.

When HA concentration was lower than 0.25 mg/L, the color of standard solution kept orange. However, while the HA concentration was higher than 0.25 mg/L, the color gradually transformed to brownish-red then to brownish-grey and blue. Therefore, this multicolor change can be easily discriminated by naked eye, and an equipment-free semiquantitative detection of HA was attained via naked-eye readout with a visible detection limit of 0.25 mg/L. Meanwhile, HA can be quantitatively determined according to the shift value of the longitudinal LSPR peak (Δλ). Fig. 4B displays that the blue shift of the longitudinal LSPR peak is gradually hampered as the concentration of HA increased. As shown in Fig. 4D, Δλ linearly correlates with the logarithm of HA concentrations from 0.25 mg/L to 2.0 mg/L. The linear equation is Δλ = 120.86–118.3[lg CHA (mg/L)] (R2 = 0.991). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the probabilistic approach recommended by IUPAC (Gan et al., 2016). The value of XL is given by the equation: XL = Xb + k Sb, where Xb is the mean value of blank measurements, Sb is the standard deviation of blank measurements, k is a numerical factor and a value of 3 is strongly recommended by IUPAC (corresponding to a confidence level of 90%). Based on this equation, LOD is calculated to be 0.2 mg/L (S/N = 3). However, it is relatively inconvenient to use Δλ to quantify HA since absorption spectrum scanning and calculation of the shift value of LSPR peak are time-consuming which remarkably prolong the detection time. In contrast, fluorometry is highly suitable for quantification due to its short response time and high sensitivity. As displayed Fig. 4C, the quenching of CDs by Au NRs@Ag is gradually inhibited with the increase of HA concentration, and there is a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 515 nm (I) and logarithm of HA concentration from 0.1 mg/L to 4.0 mg/L (Fig. 4E). The linear equation is I = 106.5 + 42.05 [lg CHA (mg/L)] (R2 = 0.996), and the LOD is 0.08 mg/L (S/N = 3). Compared with the quantification of HA using Δλ, quantification of HA using the fluorometric assay not only takes less detection time, but also exhibits broader detection range and 2.5 times improvement on sensitivity. This is because the blue-shift of the longitudinal LSPR peak and the enhancement in its intensity (Figs. 3A and 4B) due to the silver deposition on Au NRs were harnessed and translated into the increase of light absorption to the excitation/emission of CDs via IFE, thus yielding a more sensitive fluorometric assay. Therefore, the as-developed dual-modal immunosensor could realize a more reliable and precise HA detection and fit different testing needs by conducting a naked-eye semiquantitative detection based on the multicolor change and an instrumentally quantitative detection based on the fluorometric readout. Furthermore, compared with recently reported immunoassays, multicolorimetric sensors and fluorescent methods for HA detection (Table S1), this immunosensor had a unique dual-signal-readout merit and exhibited comparable or better detection performance.

To verify the specificity of the dual-modal immunosensor, 1 mg/L of HA, histidine, tryptophane, tryptamine, tyramine and phenethylamine standard solutions prepared in PBS buffer (0.01M, pH7.4), as well as a blank control (PBS buffer), were subjected to the test by the dual-modal immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 5 , the responses (both colorimetric and fluorometric) induced by these analogs were at comparable level with those induced by blank, which were much lower than those induced by HA, indicating that the proposed immunosensor had excellent selectivity for HA.

To evaluate the potential application in practical analysis, this immunosensor was applied to detect HA in codfish and pork samples via spiked experiments. It was found that the matrix effect on the development of multicolor in the designed dual-modal immunosensor was negligible after diluting by 40-fold in PBS buffer (0.01M, pH7.4). (Fig. S4). Since the MRL of HA in food permitted by the FDA is 50 mg/kg, three spiking level (20, 50 and100 mg/kg) were set at the level approximate to 50 mg/kg. Therefore, negative codfish and pork samples spiked with HA standard of three different concentrations (20, 50 and100 mg/kg), were extracted and diluted 40-fold with PBS buffer (0.01M, pH7.4) to eliminate matrix effect, then their HA contents were visually semi-quantified and instrumentally quantified based on the multi-colorimetric mode and fluorometric mode of the proposed immunosensor, respectively. For visually semiquantitative detection, the concentration of the unknown sample can be achieved by the following procedure: firstly, the color of the sample solution was compared with the standard reference card (Fig. 4A). If the color of a sample was close to the color of one of the standard solutions displayed in Fig. 4A, then the concentration of the sample was obtained via multiplying the concentration of the standard color solution by the dilution factor (40-fold); if the color of the sample was in between the colors of two standard solutions, then the concentration of the sample was achieved via multiplying the average concentration of the two standard solutions by 40 times. According to the color variation for standard solutions, a distinct color transition from orange to brownish-red, brownish-grey and blue was observed when the concentration of HA standard solution increased from 0.25 mg/kg to 0.4 mg/kg, 0.75 mg/kg and 1.25 mg/kg. This means the HA content variation in real samples (codfish and pork) from 10 mg/kg to 16 mg/kg, 30 mg/kg and 50 mg/kg can be easily discriminated via naked-eye readout. As presented in Table 1 , the HA concentrations of the six HA-spiked samples could be accurately judged from the colors of the corresponding assay solutions. For quantitative detection, recoveries of the added HA in the six samples were determined to be in the range of 81.3–101% with the RSDs less than 10% (Table 1), and the obtained results correlated well with those of LC-MS/MS. These results indicate the good accuracy and precision of the fluorometric readout. Since the maximum residue level (MRL) of HA in foods varied from 50 to 1000 mg/kg, we can adjust the dilution factor to appropriate level during sample pretreatment according to the MRL adopted during practical application. Therefore, a naked-eye readout via this immunosensor shows great potential for applying in on-site screening HA positive samples, results of which can be further confirmed by fluorometric redout via this immunosensor.

In summary, by integrating the IFE of CDs induced by an ALP-enabled metallization of Au NRs with ELISA platform, a novel immunosensor with multi-colorimetric and fluorometric dual-readout was developed for the precise detection of HA. In this dual-modal immunosensor, the product of ALP-enabled metallization of Au NRs reaction, Au NRs@Ag, act as both the colorimetric indicator for multi-colorimetric assay and the absorber in IFE-based fluorometric assay; meanwhile, CDs serve as the fluorophore. This immunosensor not only inherits the advantage of high resolution and accuracy in naked-eye semiquantitative detection from the Au NRs-based multi-colorimetric assay, but also the merit of superior suitability and sensitivity in quantitative detection from the IFE-based fluorometric assay, which make this method suitable for both laboratory and on-site detection. Moreover, the independent dual-signal transduction mechanism can remarkably enhance the detection precision and reliability. Accordingly, we envision that this proposed dual-modal immunosensor could become a potent tool for detection of other food contaminants, and create an avenue to construct more versatile immunoassay platforms as well.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Shuang-Zi Luo: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jin-Yi Yang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Bao-Zhu Jia: Investigation, Methodology. Hong Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Zi-Jian Chen: Investigation, Methodology. Yu-Dong Shen: Visualization, Methodology. Hong-Tao Lei: Supervision, Methodology. Zhen-Lin Xu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lin Luo: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31822039, 31801621), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021B1515120042), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2021A1515012422), Guangzhou Planned Program in Science and Technology (202102021002).
